title: The paradoxes of populism
gist: Yglesias downplays the DSA's New York primary wins as legislatively meaningless (the losers were already very left-wing), highlights a moderate counter-movement signing up eight new members of Congress, and uses the 'stealth democracy' framework to explain populist paradoxes - voters reject the idea that political conflict can stem from genuine tradeoffs among people of goodwill, attributing it instead to corruption.

themes.theme: DSA wins are organizational flex, not policy change

insights.claim: The DSA's New York primary wins are an impressive organizational flex but legislatively inconsequential because the incumbents they beat were themselves very left-wing, with no big pro-Israel outliers among the losers and noted moderate Ritchie Torres winning easily.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Headlines about a leftist surge overstate the policy shift; replacing left incumbents with slightly-more-left ones changes little - discount the DSA wins as a directional signal.
insights.quote: replacing House members who are to the left of the median Democrat with members who are somewhat further left doesn't actually change anything
insights.locator: What I do want to say

insights.claim: The Adam Gray/Tom Suozzi 'Promise to America' moderate movement signed up eight new members of Congress including tough-seat holders Vicente Gonzalez, Don Davis, and Laura Gillen, plus electoral superstar Kristen McDonald Rivet and throwback rural candidate Jamie Ager in North Carolina.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A coordinated moderate bloc expanding in competitive seats is the more electorally consequential development than the DSA wins drawing the headlines.
insights.quote: the Adam Gray/Tom Suozzi Promise to America movement signed up eight new members of Congress
insights.locator: Another thing that happened this week

themes.theme: After leftist failure comes the pro-growth reformer

insights.claim: The leftist insurgency stopped talking about Chicago because the left already won power there and voters hated it, and a wave of pro-growth reformist mayors like Matt Mahan and Dan Lurie in the Bay Area represents what emerges after left-wing failures.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Urban political cycles tend to swing from leftist governance to pro-growth reform after failure - a pattern to watch in cities currently run by the left.
insights.quote: the left already won power in Chicago and voters have hated it, so the left doesn't talk about Chicago anymore
insights.locator: I saw some speculation on Twitter

insights.claim: Even a leftist mayor can succeed by following the Zohran Mamdani path of keeping a predecessor's crime and education policies, not raising taxes on residents or businesses, and focusing on zoning reform.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Pro-growth, tax-stable, zoning-focused governance is the survival template even for left mayors - a tell for which left-run cities avoid the failure cycle.
insights.quote: keeping his predecessor's key policies on crime and education in place, not raising taxes on city residents or city businesses, and focusing on zoning reform
insights.locator: you can always pursue the Zohran Mamdani path

insights.claim: The 'stealth democracy' thesis of Theiss-Morse and Hibbing holds that most of the public is fundamentally hostile to normal democratic-republic operation, viewing sharp partisan conflict as evidence that key actors are not on the level rather than as legitimate disagreement.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A predictive lens on voter behavior: the electorate reads gridlock as corruption, which explains support for outsider/populist anti-establishment messaging.
insights.quote: voters view the existence of sharp partisan conflict as evidence that key actors in the system are not on the level

insights.claim: Because voters reject that political conflict can arise from genuine tradeoffs among people of goodwill, they reconcile tradeoff-aversion with a zero-sum worldview by attributing disagreement to corrupt or malign forces appropriating resources - hence zero-sum views of immigrants, billionaires, USAID, or aid to Israel.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Explains the durability of scapegoat politics: voters expect that eliminating a 'malign' actor will make tradeoffs vanish, a belief politicians can repeatedly exploit.
insights.quote: What can be the case is that corrupt or malign forces are appropriating resources to clearly illegitimate ends.
insights.locator: Notably, this reconciles the aversion to acknowledging tradeoffs
